name: Tanngnjost
description: Tanngnjost, meaning 'tooth-grinder,' is one of the two goats that pull the chariot of the thunder god Thor across the sky. Along with his brother Tanngrisnir ('tooth-gnasher'), Tanngnjost provides a vital service to Thor, allowing him to traverse the realms with speed and power. These goats are not merely beasts of burden; they possess the remarkable ability to be slaughtered and eaten, only to be resurrected whole the next day, provided their bones remain unbroken. This ability is showcased in several myths where Thor and his companions rely on the goats for sustenance during their travels. The protection of these goats is paramount, as any harm inflicted upon their bones prevents their resurrection. They represent a source of renewable food and transportation for the god of thunder, highlighting the interconnectedness of the divine and the natural world in Norse cosmology. Their existence underscores the themes of sacrifice, renewal, and the importance of respecting the natural order. Tanngnjost and Tanngrisnir are essential components of Thor's powerful image and the overall narrative of Norse mythology. They are a testament to the ingenuity and symbolic richness of the ancient Norse worldview, where even everyday creatures could hold significant power and meaning.
subtitle: Thor's goat, 'tooth-grinder'

keyMyths.title: Thor's Feast
keyMyths.description: Thor and Loki stay at a peasant's home and slaughter Tanngnjost and Tanngrisnir for food. Loki convinces the peasant's son, Thjálfi, to break one of the goat's leg bones to suck the marrow.

keyMyths.title: The Consequences of Thjálfi's Act
keyMyths.description: The next morning, Tanngnjost is resurrected, but is lame due to the broken bone. As penance, Thor takes Thjálfi and his sister Röskva as his servants.

keyMyths.title: Thor's Journey
keyMyths.description: Tanngnjost and Tanngrisnir pull Thor's chariot across the sky, allowing him to travel quickly to various locations in the realms, fighting giants and protecting Asgard.
